planting.note: Plant bare-root or container stock in late winter or spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich, moderately fertile soil in full sun to part shade; mayhaw is native to the river bottomlands and swampy depressions of the Gulf Coastal Plain from east Texas to the Carolinas — it thrives in seasonally wet soils and is the source of the most beloved traditional preserve of the Deep South, mayhaw jelly.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in full sun to part shade; mayhaw naturally grows in wet bottomlands and tolerates seasonal flooding. Crataegus opaca (Western mayhaw or May haw) is a native hawthorn of the Gulf Coastal Plain — a small to medium deciduous tree growing in the wet, seasonally flooded bottomlands, bayous, and boggy depressions of eastern Texas, Louisiana, Mississippi, Alabama, and Georgia. It is the source of one of the most prized and distinctively Southern traditional preserves: mayhaw jelly, made from the small (1/2-inch), bright-red to orange-red, apple-flavored fruits that ripen in April and May (hence 'mayhaw' — haw ripening in May). Mayhaw jelly has a uniquely mild, apple-quince-like flavor with subtle floral notes that makes it a beloved regional specialty — spread on hot biscuits, it represents the taste of the Gulf South spring. The fruits must be harvested from the ground or water (they drop when ripe, sometimes into shallow water) and are processed immediately — too sour and dry to eat fresh, but exceptional for jellies, preserves, and syrup. Many improved fruiting cultivars have been selected in Louisiana, Texas, and Georgia; 'Super Berry,' 'Texas Star,' and 'Crimson' are productive modern selections. Mayhaw is notably tolerant of wet soils and occasional flooding, making it valuable for rain gardens and wet spots in Gulf Coast landscapes.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Mayhaw Production
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Mayhaw
USDA Plants Database — Crataegus opaca

subcategory: tropical seed and leaf edible tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; melinjo is a remarkable tropical tree distantly related to conifers rather than flowering plants — one of very few gymnosperms with edible seeds and leaves — the seeds are the basis of emping crackers (kerupuk melinjo), one of Indonesia's most distinctive traditional snack foods.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; melinjo grows slowly and tolerates light shade when young. Gnetum gnemon (melinjo or paddy oat) is one of the most botanically unusual food plants in cultivation: it belongs to the Gnetaceae, a group of gymnosperms (cone-bearing plants related to conifers) rather than flowering plants — making it one of very few non-angiosperm food trees (alongside ginko, cycads, and pines). Despite looking superficially like a tropical flowering tree with broad, opposite, shiny green leaves and red seed pods, it is more closely related to conifers than to any fruit tree. In Indonesia and Malaysia, both the leaves and seeds of melinjo are important food ingredients. The young leaves are used in Javanese vegetable stews (sayur asem) and soups; the seeds, after roasting and pounding flat, are fried to make emping melinjo (also called emping crackers or gnemon chips) — thin, translucent, slightly bitter crackers that are a standard accompaniment to Indonesian nasi goreng, rijsttafel, and as a snack. The ripe red fruits (technically the sarcotesta, a fleshy seed coat rather than true fruit) are boiled and the starchy, mildly bitter seeds inside are peeled, pressed flat, and sun-dried before frying. CAUTION: High in purines — not recommended for gout sufferers.
sources: USDA Agricultural Research Service — Gnetum gnemon
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Gnetum
Indonesian Ministry of Agriculture — Melinjo

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in part shade to full shade; mountain pepper is an Australian native highland shrub from Tasmania and the alpine regions of southeast Australia — the berries and dried leaves are used as an intensely hot, aromatic, native Australian spice and are one of the signature ingredients of modern Australian native cuisine.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in part shade to full shade; mulch heavily to keep roots cool and moist. Tasmannia lanceolata (mountain pepper or Tasmanian pepperberry) is a native Australian alpine shrub from the cold, moist rainforests and alpine heathlands of Tasmania, Victoria, and New South Wales — the most important native Australian spice plant and a signature ingredient of contemporary Australian native cuisine. Both the black, ripe berries and the dried, ground leaves are intensely peppery-hot, with a heat that is initially sharp and then evolves into a prolonged, warming burn distinct from black pepper's heat — the primary pungent compounds (polygodial and drimanyl acetate) belong to a different chemical class from piperine (black pepper's heat compound) and interact differently with the tongue. The dried leaves have a complex, eucalyptus-clove-pepper aroma and are used in Australia as a seasoning rub for lamb, game, and seafood; the berries are used whole or crushed as a distinctive peppercorn substitute. Mountain pepper has become a centerpiece of the Australian 'bush tucker' (native food) movement and is increasingly available in gourmet food markets internationally. The plant is dioecious — plant male and female shrubs for berry production. In cultivation outside Australia, it requires cool, moist, acidic, shaded conditions.
sources: RIRDC Australia — Native Spice Crops
Australian Native Food Industry — Tasmannia
Royal Botanic Gardens Victoria — Mountain Pepper

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in part shade to full shade; Ganges Primrose is a Southeast Asian Strobilanthes producing nearly continuous pale lavender-blue flowers on a shrubby, semi-sprawling plant — it performs in conditions similar to Persian Shield (S. dyerianus) but is grown primarily for flowers rather than foliage.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full shade; pinch to encourage bushiness and more prolific flowering. Strobilanthes isophyllus (Ganges primrose or India snapweed) is a shrubby, semi-sprawling tropical perennial from India and Southeast Asia in the acanthus family (Acanthaceae), producing a nearly continuous display of pale lavender-blue to soft violet, snapdragon-like (tubular, two-lipped) flowers from spring through fall in warm climates. The plant is grown primarily for its prolific flowering rather than its foliage — the leaves are glossy, lance-shaped, and dark green, attractive but not as dramatic as those of the related Persian Shield (S. dyerianus). Strobilanthes isophyllus tolerates a range of light conditions from bright indirect to quite deep shade, where it continues to flower more readily than many shade plants. In zone 9–12, it grows as a perennial; in colder climates, it makes an excellent container plant that can be overwintered indoors. The similar 'Gossip Girl' and 'Kimberley Blue' cultivars are sometimes attributed to this species or close relatives. A useful and underused shade-flowering perennial for warm, humid climates where few ornamentals bloom reliably in full shade.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Strobilanthes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Flowering Plants
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder

planting.note: Plant stem cuttings in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; Veldt grape is an African and Asian tropical succulent vine with square, segmented, succulent green stems and small deciduous leaves — it is one of the most important plants in Ayurvedic medicine for bone and joint conditions, used for centuries to treat fractures, osteoporosis, and joint pain.

directions: Plant stem section cuttings in well-drained, lean, dry soil in full sun to part shade; water sparingly — Cissus quadrangularis is drought-tolerant and susceptible to root rot in wet soils. Cissus quadrangularis (veldt grape or harjora) is a tropical African and Asian succulent vine that looks unlike any typical grape-family plant (Vitaceae) — it produces thick, fleshy, distinctly four-winged-square (quadrangular = four-cornered) green stems segmented at intervals like bamboo nodes, with small, deciduous, kidney-shaped leaves and tiny clusters of red berries at the nodes. The plant is primarily known not as a food or ornamental but as one of the most important plants in Ayurvedic medicine — it is called 'harjora' in Sanskrit (literally 'bone-joiner') and is used for more than 2,000 years to accelerate fracture healing, treat osteoporosis, joint pain, and as a bone-strengthening tonic. Scientific studies have confirmed that extracts of C. quadrangularis stimulate osteoblast (bone cell) activity and accelerate bone mineralization. It is widely sold as a supplement in India and, increasingly, in Western natural health markets. In the garden in zone 9–12, Veldt grape is an ornamental curiosity — the square, succulent, bright-green stems and climbing or trailing habit make it an unusual specimen for a succulent or tropical garden.
sources: Indian Council of Agricultural Research — Cissus quadrangularis
Journal of Ethnopharmacology — Cissus medicinal properties
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Succulents

planting.note: Direct sow or plant divisions in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; punarnava is a wide-ranging tropical weed and Ayurvedic medicinal plant of India and the tropics whose edible leaves, roots, and seeds have been used for centuries as a liver tonic, diuretic, and kidney-support herb.

directions: Direct sow in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; punarnava spreads readily by seed and can naturalize in warm climates. Boerhavia diffusa (punarnava or red spiderling) is a tropical prostrate or spreading perennial herb found throughout the tropics and subtropics of India, Africa, Asia, and the Americas — simultaneously one of the most widespread tropical 'weeds' and one of the most important medicinal plants in Ayurvedic medicine. In Ayurveda, punarnava ('the one that renews the body') is one of the top-ranked diuretic, liver-protective, and kidney-supporting herbs, with documented clinical studies supporting its hepatoprotective (liver-protective) and anti-inflammatory effects. The leaves are also eaten as a pot herb in India and West Africa — boiled like spinach, with a mildly mucilaginous texture and bland-earthy flavor. The plant produces tiny, attractive, magenta-pink flowers throughout the warm season, followed by small, glandular, sticky seeds. As a garden plant in zone 9–12, punarnava is a low, spreading, self-seeding herb that naturalizes readily in disturbed areas and lean soils. It tolerates drought and poor soils that few other herbs can survive. The red-stemmed spreading growth creates a soft, groundcover-like effect in dry garden areas. An important herb for those interested in Ayurvedic herbal practice.
sources: Indian Council of Agricultural Research — Medicinal Plants
American Botanical Council — Boerhavia diffusa
USDA PLANTS Database — Boerhavia diffusa

planting.note: Direct sow or allow to self-seed in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in part shade to full shade; shiny bush is a tiny, succulent-stemmed, heart-leaved tropical herb that grows in moist, shaded conditions and is entirely edible — the mild, slightly peppery, watercress-like leaves and stems are used fresh in salads and as a cooked vegetable throughout Southeast Asia, the Philippines, and tropical Latin America.

directions: Direct sow or transplant seedlings in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in shade to part shade; allow to self-seed for a continuous supply. Peperomia pellucida (shiny bush or clearweed) is a tiny, semi-succulent tropical annual or short-lived perennial herb — a member of the pepper family (Piperaceae) related to black pepper — native to tropical South and Central America but naturalized throughout the tropical world in shaded, moist, disturbed areas, including the floors of tropical markets, along walls, and in garden beds. The plant produces translucent-stemmed, bright-green, heart-shaped, succulent leaves just 1–2 inches across with a mild, slightly peppery, watercress-like flavor and juicy, tender texture when fresh. The entire plant — leaves, stems, and all — is edible raw in salads or lightly cooked as a pot herb; in the Philippines (where it is called 'pansit-pansitan'), it is eaten fresh as a salad green and cooked in soups; in Brazil (where it is called 'erva-de-vidro') and throughout Latin America, it is used as a mild culinary herb and folk remedy for arthritis and skin conditions. Peperomia pellucida is one of the easiest and most useful shade herbs for warm, humid gardens: it self-seeds abundantly, grows in deep shade under trees where little else will grow, and provides a continuous harvest of mild salad greens from a minimal footprint.
sources: World Vegetable Center — Peperomia pellucida
Philippine Department of Agriculture — Pansit-Pansitan
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Peperomia pellucida
